claims.claim_text: Aeration alone accounts for approximately 45 to 60% of total facility electricity use in municipal wastewater treatment.
source_chunk_ids: Zohrabian et al. - 2021 - Leveraging the water-energy nexus to derive benefits for the electric grid through demand-side manag_K E Y W O R D S_1_30

claims.claim_text: The energy intensity of wastewater treatment, combined with the growing need for flexible demand-side resources to support electric grids with high penetrations of variable renewable generation, has motivated research into leveraging wastewater treatment plants as participants in demand response programs.
source_chunk_ids: Zohrabian et al. - 2021 - Leveraging the water-energy nexus to derive benefits for the electric grid through demand-side manag_Abstract_0_28
Zohrabian et al. - 2021 - Leveraging the water-energy nexus to derive benefits for the electric grid through demand-side manag_K E Y W O R D S_0_29

claims.claim_text: The simulation platforms most widely used to evaluate aeration control strategies—chief among them the Benchmark Simulation Model No. 1 (BSM1)—produce performance metrics that are standardized but not directly monetary.
source_chunk_ids: Machado et al. - 2020 - Systematic comparison framework for selecting the best retrofitting alternative for an existing wate_EDITOR'S CHOICE— RESEARCH ARTICLE_0_81

claims.claim_text: BSM1 and its extensions employ two aggregate indices to assess plant performance: the Effluent Quality Index (EQI), which weights effluent concentrations of total suspended solids, chemical oxygen demand, biochemical oxygen demand, total Kjeldahl nitrogen, and nitrate into a single mass-based score, and the Operational Cost Index (OCI), which aggregates aeration energy, pumping energy, sludge production, and other operating expenditures into a dimensionless daily cost surrogate.
source_chunk_ids: Machado et al. - 2020 - Systematic comparison framework for selecting the best retrofitting alternative for an existing wate_EDITOR'S CHOICE— RESEARCH ARTICLE_0_81

claims.claim_text: The EQI weighting factors originate from regulatory traditions—most notably the European Community Urban Waste Water Treatment Directive (EEC Council, 1991)—that impose concentration ceilings on pollutant discharges rather than continuous price schedules.
source_chunk_ids: Machado et al. - 2020 - Systematic comparison framework for selecting the best retrofitting alternative for an existing wate_EDITOR'S CHOICE— RESEARCH ARTICLE_0_79

claims.claim_text: Stare et al. (2007) introduced a piecewise-linear effluent fine formulation in which each pollutant j incurs a cost that increases at a mild slope when the effluent concentration remains below the discharge limit, and at a steeper slope once the limit is exceeded—activated by a Heaviside step function.
source_chunk_ids: Machado et al. - 2020 - Systematic comparison framework for selecting the best retrofitting alternative for an existing wate_EDITOR'S CHOICE— RESEARCH ARTICLE_0_81

claims.claim_text: The two-slope fine structure has been adopted and extended in subsequent studies by Machado et al. (2020) and Vanrolleghem and Gillot (2002), producing daily effluent fines in euros that enter a total operating cost expression alongside aeration energy costs at a conversion factor of 0.1 €/kWh and sludge treatment costs at 0.5 €/kg.
source_chunk_ids: Machado et al. - 2020 - Systematic comparison framework for selecting the best retrofitting alternative for an existing wate_EDITOR'S CHOICE— RESEARCH ARTICLE_0_81

claims.claim_text: Garibay-Rodriguez et al. (2017) formulated two alternative penalization architectures at the watershed scale: a proportional fine per unit mass of pollutant discharged above the environmental constraint and a fixed fine triggered by any degree of regulatory violation.
source_chunk_ids: Garibay-Rodriguez et al. - 2017 - Optimal water management in macroscopic systems under economic penalty scenarios_Economic penalizations_0_45
Garibay-Rodriguez et al. - 2017 - Optimal water management in macroscopic systems under economic penalty scenarios_Economic penalizations_0_52

claims.claim_text: The magnitude of the fine parameter φ was shown through sensitivity analysis to govern the economic–environmental trade-off decisively.
source_chunk_ids: Garibay-Rodriguez et al. - 2017 - Optimal water management in macroscopic systems under economic penalty scenarios_Sensitivity analysis with respect to parameter /_0_64

claims.claim_text: Reducing or interrupting aeration to shed electrical load during peak price periods can degrade nitrification performance, elevate ammonia concentrations, and increase effluent turbidity.
source_chunk_ids: Zohrabian et al. - 2021 - Leveraging the water-energy nexus to derive benefits for the electric grid through demand-side manag_K E Y W O R D S_1_30

claims.claim_text: Pilot studies have confirmed that while some facilities tolerate short aeration interruptions without measurable quality deterioration—particularly those with supplementary oxygenation sources—others experience immediate ammonia breakthroughs that would trigger regulatory fines.
source_chunk_ids: Zohrabian et al. - 2021 - Leveraging the water-energy nexus to derive benefits for the electric grid through demand-side manag_K E Y W O R D S_1_30

claims.claim_text: The economic question of DR participation becomes one of balancing the marginal energy cost savings from DR participation against the marginal increase in effluent penalty, a structure that naturally lends itself to equilibrium or optimization formulations where the first-order conditions equate marginal benefits and marginal costs.
source_chunk_ids: Garibay-Rodriguez et al. - 2017 - Optimal water management in macroscopic systems under economic penalty scenarios_Objective function_0_54
Garibay-Rodriguez et al. - 2017 - Optimal water management in macroscopic systems under economic penalty scenarios_Comparison of solutions at unrestricted FWC_0_62

claims.claim_text: The pollutant-specific weights embedded in the EQI derive from European and North American regulatory frameworks that set concentration ceilings rather than continuous damage functions.
source_chunk_ids: Machado et al. - 2020 - Systematic comparison framework for selecting the best retrofitting alternative for an existing wate_EDITOR'S CHOICE— RESEARCH ARTICLE_0_79
Xenarios and Bithas - 2012 - The Use of Environmental Policy Instruments for Urban Wastewater Control Evidences from an Internat_Introduction_0_4

claims.claim_text: The family of piecewise-linear, Heaviside-switched, and fixed-fine formulations developed by Stare et al. (2007), Machado et al. (2020), and Garibay-Rodriguez et al. (2017) constitute the primary mechanisms by which simulation-derived effluent concentrations are translated into cost terms amenable to optimization.
source_chunk_ids: Machado et al. - 2020 - Systematic comparison framework for selecting the best retrofitting alternative for an existing wate_EDITOR'S CHOICE— RESEARCH ARTICLE_0_81

Garibay-Rodriguez et al. - 2017 - Optimal water management in macroscopic systems under economic penalty scenarios_Economic penalizations_0_52

claims.claim_text: Research on load shedding, load shifting, and intermittent aeration in wastewater treatment plants—reviewed comprehensively by Zohrabian et al. (2021)—establishes the operational constraints, quality risks, and energy savings magnitudes that any penalty-aware DR strategy must accommodate.
source_chunk_ids: Zohrabian et al. - 2021 - Leveraging the water-energy nexus to derive benefits for the electric grid through demand-side manag_K E Y W O R D S_0_29
Zohrabian et al. - 2021 - Leveraging the water-energy nexus to derive benefits for the electric grid through demand-side manag_K E Y W O R D S_1_30

claims.claim_text: Multi-objective and penalty-based optimization formulations, including the ε-constraint approaches and sensitivity analyses of fine parameters explored by Garibay-Rodriguez et al. (2017), provide the mathematical scaffolding for identifying cost-minimizing operating points that respect both energy market signals and effluent quality constraints.
